title: Lambda Function Secrets Access — execution role scoping, Secrets Manager, KMS
description: An engineer is writing a Lambda function that needs read access to a database password in Secrets Manager, encrypted by the `AccountFoundation.kmsRing.secrets` CMK. Target end state: the Lambda execution role has the narrowest possible grant (`secretsmanager:GetSecretValue` on exactly one ARN), KMS key policy grants `kms:Decrypt` to that role only, environment variables do not leak sensitive config, VPC execution is used when the Lambda needs to reach private resources, and dead-letter queues + X-Ray tracing are on for observability.
actors: Lambda execution role (trusted within its narrow scope)
External Attacker (via RCE in the Lambda code or via Lambda URL)
assets: The database password stored in Secrets Manager
The KMS CMK that encrypts the secret
Lambda function code and layer contents

stride.name: Over-scoped execution role
stride.description: An LLM-authored Lambda policy grants `secretsmanager:GetSecretValue` with `Resource: '*'` or `kms:Decrypt` on all keys. Any post-deployment compromise of the Lambda exposes every secret in the account. Mitigation: exact-ARN policy; HardeningPack rejects wildcard `Resource` outside narrow read namespaces.

stride.name: Secret leakage via environment variable
stride.description: Developers pass the raw secret into the Lambda's `environment.variables` instead of fetching it at invocation. The secret ends up in CloudFormation state, CloudTrail `UpdateFunctionConfiguration`, and Lambda console display. Mitigation: runtime fetch via SDK; CrossGuard rule rejecting env vars matching password/secret/key patterns.

stride.name: Supply-chain dependency tampering
stride.description: A Node / Python dependency in the Lambda's `node_modules` / `site-packages` becomes malicious (Shai-Hulud-style npm account takeover). Mitigation: exact-version pinning + integrity hashes in `package-lock.json` / `poetry.lock`; SLSA provenance verification in the Lambda packaging pipeline.

stride.name: Prompt-injection-driven secret exfiltration
stride.description: If the Lambda handles user prompts for an LLM-backed workflow, a malicious user can craft a prompt that coerces the LLM to produce code that fetches and emits the Secret Manager secret — a direct exfil channel. Mitigation: strict output parsing, no `eval` of LLM output, deny-list on sensitive secret names.

stride.name: Repudiation via non-existent Lambda invocation logs
stride.description: Lambda is invoked from Lambda URL or API Gateway; without CloudWatch Logs + X-Ray, invocation-level attribution is impossible. Mitigation: CloudWatch Logs on, retention ≥ 90d, X-Ray tracing on.

stride.name: Lambda cost-DoS via unthrottled concurrency
stride.description: An attacker with the Lambda URL triggers millions of invocations, exhausting Lambda concurrency and driving cost. Mitigation: reserved concurrency cap, API Gateway rate-limits, AWS WAF in front.

recommendedComponents.name: hulumi.baseline.aws.SecureLambda
recommendedComponents.availability: Planned for v1.1+ (post-v1.0.0; not yet shipped)
recommendedComponents.rationale: Planned ComponentResource wrapping AWS Lambda with least-privilege execution role, VPC default, DLQ wiring, X-Ray tracing, reserved concurrency, and env-var secret-name denylist.

recommendedComponents.name: hulumi.baseline.aws.AccountFoundation.secretsManager + kmsRing.secrets
recommendedComponents.rationale: Hulumi's AccountFoundation scaffolds a Secrets Manager namespace + a `secrets` CMK with rotation. The Lambda's execution role policy references the specific secret ARN and the specific key ARN, not wildcards.

recommendedComponents.name: hulumi.policies.aws.HulumiHardeningPack
recommendedComponents.rationale: Blocks wildcard IAM and env-var secret exposure; rejects raw `aws.lambda.Function` without a supplied `role` that the pack can inspect.
